title: Understanding STIs: Types and Long-Term Effects
description: What is a STI? STIs are infections spread through sex or intimate contact. Common STIs: • Chlamydia • Gonorrhea • Syphilis • HIV • Herpes • Hepatitis B • HPV Long-term effects if untreated: Fertility & gynecological issues: Infections like chlamydia and gonorrhea can cause pelvic pain, infertility, or ectopic pregnancies. Genital or anal issues: HPV and herpes can lead to warts, cancer, or scarring. Overall health: HIV, hepatitis, and syphilis can damage organs, cause cancer, or lead to AIDS.

title: How STIs Spread and Ways to Prevent Them
description: How are STIs transmitted? STIs can be spread through skin-to-skin contact, kissing, or any kind of sex (oral, vaginal, or anal) involving body fluids like blood, semen, or saliva. How to prevent STIs? Vaccines can prevent hepatitis A and B and HPV. Using condoms or dental dams during sex reduces the risk of most STIs. PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) is available to prevent HIV transmission for those at higher risk. Be sure to check the condom's expiry date, avoid using 2 condoms at once, and use proper lubricants. Are STIs treatable? Many STIs are treatable with antibiotics, but some like herpes and HIV can't be cured, only managed. Antibiotic resistance is making infections like gonorrhea harder to treat.

title: Getting Tested for STIs: When, How, and Why
description: When should you get tested? Get tested if you or your partner have been sexually active with others, had unprotected sex, or shared needles. Also, if you or your partner show STI symptoms, it's important to get tested. What to expect during your testing appointment? Your healthcare provider may ask about your sexual activity, take urine or blood samples, and use swabs from areas like the throat or genitals. They may also do an external genital check and, if applicable, a cervical cancer screening or bimanual exam. How do you tell your partner you have an STI? It's important that your partner(s) get tested too. There are tools and programs available to help you inform them, including anonymous options. Contact your public health department for assistance.

title: HIV & Hepatitis B
description: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) What is it? HIV attacks the immune system and can lead to AIDS if untreated. How do you get it? HIV spreads through unprotected sex, contact with infected blood, or sharing needles. It is not transmitted through casual contact. With proper treatment, the risk of sexual transmission is negligible. PrEP can help prevent HIV, but it does not protect against other STIs. How can you tell if you have it? Some have no symptoms, while others may experience mild flu-like symptoms 2 to 4 weeks after exposure. How do you get tested? HIV is diagnosed with a blood test, which can detect the virus 3 to 4 weeks after infection. Seek medical attention if you suspect exposure. How is it treated? There is no cure, but antiretroviral medications help manage the virus, allowing for a longer, healthier life and preventing sexual transmission when the viral load is undetectable. Hepatitis B What is it? A virus that infects the liver and can cause serious damage. How do you get it? Spread through unprotected sex, sharing contaminated drug-use equipment, or unsterile medical procedures. Prevention Vaccination against hepatitis B can provide protection. How can you tell if you have it? Symptoms may include tiredness, abdominal pain, dark urine, or jaundice, but many show no signs. How do you get tested? Testing is done via blood tests. How is it treated? Most recover within six months. Antibody injections may be given after exposure, along with the vaccine.

title: Hepatitis C & HPV
description: Hepatitis C What is it? Hepatitis C is a liver disease caused by the hepatitis C virus (HCV), leading to liver damage and cancer. No vaccine exists. How do you get it? Spread via infected blood, often through shared drug-use equipment, unsterile medical/tattoo tools, and personal items. Sexual transmission is rare but possible. Symptoms Most have no symptoms. If present: tiredness, abdominal pain, dark urine, nausea, or jaundice. Testing A blood test confirms it. Discuss other STI testing with a healthcare provider. Treatment Some clear the virus naturally; others need medication. Chronic hepatitis C can be cured but reinfection is possible. Human papillomavirus (HPV) What is it? HPV infects different body parts, causing warts or cell changes leading to cancers. How do you get it? Through skin-to-skin contact during sexual activity. Symptoms Often none. Warts on genitals or anus, detectable by visual exam or Pap test. HPV vaccine Recommended for ages 9-26, beneficial even if sexually active. Testing Pap tests detect cervical changes. No HPV test exists for penises. Treatment HPV can’t be cured. Warts may go away on their own or need removal (freezing, ointment, surgery). Reinfection is possible.

title: Syphilis & Gonorrhea
description: Syphilis What is it? Syphilis is a bacterial infection. Undetected, it can be passed on in the first 2 years, causing serious health problems if untreated. How do you get it? Spread through condomless sex and from mother to baby during pregnancy or childbirth. Testing during pregnancy is crucial. Symptoms Symptoms may include: • A painless sore (chancre) on genitals, anus, or throat that goes away but does not mean the infection is gone. • Rash or flu-like symptoms that also resolve. • Swollen lymph nodes. Testing Tested with a blood test or swab of sores. Discuss additional STI testing, including HIV. Treatment Cured with antibiotics; follow-up blood tests are required. Gonorrhea What is it? Gonorrhea is a bacterial infection often occurring with chlamydia, on the rise and increasingly antibiotic-resistant. Untreated, it can cause infertility and pelvic inflammatory disease. How do you get it? Spread through condomless sex. Pregnant individuals should get tested to avoid passing it to the baby. Symptoms Often asymptomatic. If symptoms occur: For vaginas: • Increased vaginal discharge • Bleeding between periods • Pain during vaginal sex For penises: • Burning sensation while urinating • Discharge from the penis Testing Tested with a urine sample or swabs. Discuss additional STI testing, including HIV. Treatment Treatment is challenging due to resistance. Take medication as prescribed and consult if symptoms persist.

title: Chlamydia & Herpes
description: Chlamydia What is it? A common bacterial STI, especially in ages 15-24. Often asymptomatic, but untreated can lead to infertility and pelvic inflammatory disease. How do you get it? Spread through condomless sex. Pregnant individuals should get tested to protect the baby. Symptoms If present: For vaginas: • Increased discharge • Itching • Bleeding • Pain during sex • Lower abdominal pain • Burning while urinating For penises: • Burning while urinating • Discharge • Itching around the penis • Testicular pain Testing Urine sample or swab; discuss other STI tests with your provider. Treatment Cured with antibiotics; recurrence possible if re-exposed. Genital Herpes What is it? An infection caused by HSV. Type 1 causes cold sores; type 2 typically leads to genital herpes. How do you get it? Spread through condomless sex, even without sores. Symptoms Many are asymptomatic. Initial outbreaks may include: • Itchiness • Painful sores • Swollen groin glands • Flu-like symptoms Recurrences Unpredictable; triggered by stress, illness, sun exposure, or menstruation. Prevention • Avoid sex with tingling sensations. • Don’t have oral sex with a cold sore. • Wait until sores heal. • Use condoms or dental dams to lower risk. Testing Swab from sores; postpone if no sores are present. Management • Keep the area clean and dry. • Avoid ointments. • Wear cotton underwear and loose clothing. • Pat dry after urination. • Use warm water for pain relief. Treatment Cannot be cured but can be managed with medications.
